top_pain_points.signal: Frustration with products that require constant app use for basic functions instead of direct control.
Irritation with aggressive subscription service promotions forced by the manufacturer.
Inconvenience and inefficiency in multi-page scanning workflows due to poor software integration.
Mental and emotional fatigue from constantly assessing social environments for safety regarding gender expression.
Frustration with social stereotypes and boxes that limit personal expression.

Difficulty in analyzing large qualitative datasets, especially calculating total volume of codes across many transcripts, requiring cumbersome workarounds.
Software being awkward to use and frustrating, especially for novices, requiring external resources like online manuals to figure out.
Fear of unreliable tools for critical tasks like scanning negatives, leading to poor results and wasted money.
Being forced to use physical gradebooks/planners due to institutional changes, then dealing with product flaws like easily tearing pages.
High operational costs of tools (e.g., printers using excessive ink), making them expensive to maintain.
Incompatibility or poor data exchange between tools (e.g., project imports not working perfectly, registration issues with non-US products).

top_desired_outcomes.signal: Efficient, straightforward, and direct control over product functions without mandatory app dependency.
A product that works as an integrated, cohesive system without hidden or forced monetization schemes.
Effective organization solutions that are flexible, adaptable, and make items easy to find and access.
A simple, motivating tool (like a checklist) to help complete tasks and provide a sense of accomplishment.
A desire for social environments where personal identity doesn't require constant conscious or unconscious adjustment for safety or acceptance.
Accurate and fast data counting with minimal manual effort, eliminating double-counting and errors.
Seamless data exchange and compatibility between different software tools (e.g., CAQDAS) for flexible workflows.
Transparent and efficient coding/analysis of qualitative data with automated calculations and pattern discovery.
High-quality outputs (e.g., scans, prints) that are reliable and professional, matching or exceeding external service quality.
Tools that are easy to set up and use independently, without relying on external expertise.
Durable and well-designed physical products (e.g., binders, planners) that withstand daily use and aid organization.
Cost-effective operation with reasonable ongoing expenses (e.g., ink, maintenance).

evidence: Happy Planner always annoys me with their calendar start days. On the month view, the week starts on Sunday. But on the week view, they start the week on Monday. That always drives me a little batty.

They never put all of anyone’s holidays on the calendar either. If you’re Jewish, some of those holidays are there and others aren’t. If you’re Christian, some of the holidays are marked, some are not.
Don’t want an 18 month planner? Just remove the months you don’t want. Love the way you can customize the pages.
You could use all three trays separately or only use two stacked if you like. It is easy enough to customize what you need.
Just remove the months you don’t want. Love the way you can customize the pages.
I’d prefer a compass for accurate angles.
The elastic band to hold the planner shut is far too tight. It is very difficult to get it around the planner brand new.
I’d prefer a compass for accurate angles.
The pens were hit or miss though. Some were extremely smooth and perfect. Others were very scratchy and skipped.
Every single marker in the pack worked perfectly.
The pens were hit or miss though. Some were extremely smooth and perfect. Others were very scratchy and skipped.
I love the stand. It makes it so easy to keep everything organized.

purchase_criteria.criterion: Clear and upfront communication of free plan limitations (especially response caps)
Ability to pay per survey or short-term subscription (not just annual)
Reliable core functionality (email sending, verification, response collection) without bugs
Pricing that is reasonable and proportional to usage (e.g., nonprofit, one-off event)
Feature parity between mobile app and web platform

pain_points.signal: Difficulty editing questions and formatting text on mobile
Mobile app crashes, has background playback issues, and is generally buggy
Free tier severely restricts responses (e.g., 10-25) before requiring expensive upgrade
Pricing is confusing and feels predatory, with high monthly costs
Inability to contact human support, only bots
Mobile app lacks landscape mode, reducing usability in sunlight
No surveys available for some respondents, app errors on login or location
Limits on answer choices in questions (e.g., max 8 for multiple choice)

desired_outcomes.outcome: Easy-to-use survey creation interface
Reliable, bug-free mobile app experience
Clear, affordable pricing with essential features in free tier
Access to timely, legitimate surveys for respondents
Ability to distribute surveys widely (e.g., to open market)
Human customer support for complex issues
Advanced analytics and presentation features

purchase_triggers.trigger: Need to view more than 10-25 survey responses
purchase_triggers.context: When free tier limits are hit during data collection
